key_insight: Pre-fracture anemia was independently associated with a higher risk of nonunion after tibia or femur fractures, and risk rose with greater anemia burden.
clinical_relevance: Anemia may help flag fracture patients at elevated risk for impaired healing, potentially informing closer follow-up and risk stratification.
method: Retrospective cohort study using commercial and Medicare fee-for-service claims from 2016–2023.
Included adults with tibial or femoral fractures, at least 1 year of prior enrollment, and no baseline cancer.
Compared pre-fracture anemia status, anemia burden, and anemia subtypes against nonunion occurring 6–18 months after fracture.
evidence_signal: In 326,673 adults, crude nonunion incidence was 42% higher with anemia, and adjusted hazard ratios remained elevated for tibia (1.83) and femur (1.38) fractures.
watch_item: Claims-based diagnosis codes can miss clinical details such as hemoglobin levels, fracture severity, and treatment factors, so the findings support association rather than causation.
paper_title: Pre-fracture Anemia Is Associated with Nonunion Following Tibia or Femur Fractures: A Retrospective Cohort Study
